Mainland Australia is the main landmass of the Australian continent, excluding Tasmania, New Guinea, and other offshore islands.
Mainland Australia encompasses the states of New South Wales, Queensland, South Australia, Victoria, Western Australia, the Australian Capital Territory, Jervis Bay Territory, and Northern Territory.
Australia separated from the supercontinent Gondwana during the Cretaceous period around 130 million years ago, with complete separation from Antarctica occurring about 53 million years ago.
Australian Aborigines occupied mainland Australia up to 50,000 to 60,000 years ago, making them one of the oldest living populations in the world outside of Africa.
Prior to European arrival in the late 18th century, over 500 different clan groups with unique cultures, beliefs, and languages inhabited mainland Australia.
Willem Janszoon was the first documented European to encounter the Australian mainland in 1606, and James Cook made the first recorded observation of Indigenous Australians in 1770.
Australia is situated on the Indo-Australian tectonic plate and is not significantly affected by severe tectonic activity.
The Australian mainland has undergone many physical changes due to the shifting of tectonic plates, resulting in the formation of various landforms.
The four primary landform divisions of Australia are: Coastal Plains, Eastern and Southern Highlands, Central Lowlands (and Deserts), and Western and Northern Plateaus (and Basins).
Southern Highlands are located in New South Wales, the Australian Capital Territory, and Victoria.
Southern Highlands experience snowfall for more than half the year during winter due to higher altitude.
Central Lowlands occupy approximately 25% of the Australian mainland and average below 200 metres above sea level.
Three primary drainage basins of the Central Lowlands: Carpentaria Lowlands, Lake Eyre, and Murray-Darling Basin.
Lowest point on the mainland is Lake Eyre, at 50 meters below sea level.
The Western Plateau was formed from an inland sea during the Cretaceous period, approximately 144 to 65 million years ago.
Plateaus consist of a stable block of igneous and metamorphic rock up to a billion years old.
The average elevation of the Plateaus is 500 meters above sea level.
Significant features of the Plateaus include Uluru, Kata Tjuta, the Finke River, the Victorian Desert, the Great Sandy Desert, and the Gibson Desert.
Western Plateau receives minimal rainfall and appears red in color.
Northern Plateau experiences heavy rainfall from November to April.
Northern Plateau is located in a more tropical region compared to the Western Plateau.
Australia's mineral, oil, and gas resources have accumulated due to processes of uplift, erosion, and thick terrigenous deposition over millions of years.

Australia is one of the world's largest producers of iron ore, coal, bauxite, manganese, nickel, copper, gold, silver, cobalt, lead, uranium, opal, and diamond, with over 350 operating mines across the mainland states.
In 2021, a substantial portion of mainland Australia's total electricity generation came from renewable energy sources, primarily wind and solar.
As of 2024, the Liberal National opposition has never controlled the entirety of Mainland Australia at the federal and mainland state levels simultaneously.
From 1788 to 1859, Britain established six colonies on the Australian mainland which later formed six states: New South Wales, Victoria, Western Australia, South Australia, Queensland, and Tasmania.

The Australian Capital Territory was established as a compromise for the national capital, which was designated as Canberra on March 12, 1913, after years of negotiation between New South Wales and Victoria.
